claims.text: On August 4, 1945, Soviet children presented U.S. Ambassador W. Averell Harriman with a hand-carved wooden replica of the Great Seal of the United States that concealed a covert listening device.

evidence.quote:
potsdam united states ambassador william
avril harriman received a gift from a
group of sov

claims.rationale: Well-documented Cold War fact. The Great Seal bug (nicknamed 'The Thing') was presented to Harriman on Aug 4, 1945 by Young Pioneers at Artek summer camp in Crimea. Event is extensively covered in NSA declassified histories and Peter Wright's 'Spycatcher'.

claims.text: The listening device was concealed inside the wooden Great Seal carving and hung on the ambassador's office wall at Spaso House in Moscow for seven years.

evidence.quote:
n replica of
the great seal of the united states the
ambassador was so pleased with the
carving that

claims.rationale: Documented in NSA Cryptologic Spectrum (1987) and numerous signals-intelligence histories. The bug was installed in 1945 and discovered in 1952, remaining operational across five ambassadors. Established fact.

dependencies.rationale: Extends the gift-presentation claim by specifying where it ended up and for how long.

claims.text: The Thing was designed by Russian inventor Leon Theremin, also famous for the electronic musical instrument that bears his name.

evidence.quote:
the thing was created by leon
theremin a russian inventor most famous
for

claims.rationale: Well-documented. Lev Sergeyevich Termen (Leon Theremin) designed the passive resonant-cavity bug in a sharashka after his return from the U.S. to the USSR. Established in Theremin biographies (Albert Glinsky, 'Theremin: Ether Music and Espionage').

claims.text: After returning to the USSR, Theremin was imprisoned in a Siberian gulag and given the choice between hard labor in a gold mine or working in a secret scientific lab (sharashka).

evidence.quote:
siberian gulag and given a choice he
could work a gold mine breaking big
r

claims.rationale: Theremin was arrested in 1938 shortly after returning to the USSR and was sent to a Kolyma gulag, then transferred to a Tupolev-supervised sharashka (OKB-8 / TsKB-29). Documented in Glinsky's biography and declassified NKVD files. Specifics align.

dependencies.rationale: Explains the circumstances under which Theremin built the Thing — in a Soviet prison lab.

claims.text: The Thing was the world's first resonant-cavity microphone — a passive device with no internal power source.

evidence.quote:
the thing the first
resonant cavity microphone the thing was
light hollow and it was made of

claims.rationale: The Thing is the canonical first instance of a passive resonant-cavity microphone (later formalized in intelligence literature). Its design — a tuned cavity whose resonance was modulated by sound pressure on a diaphragm — is a matter of public technical record since declassification.

claims.text: The Thing was activated from outside by a Soviet van parked nearby that beamed radio waves at it — a technique the NSA later called 'illuminating' a device.

evidence.quote:
the things antenna
remotely and listen to conversations at
will once the thing was no longer

claims.rationale: The 800-MHz external illumination technique is described in NSA's 1956 Peter Wright debriefing and subsequent TSCM literature. Van-based illumination from nearby Soviet premises is the standard account.

claims.text: The Thing was discovered in 1951, six years after its installation, when a British army radio operator accidentally tuned into the KGB's relay frequency.

evidence.quote:
d
until
in 1951 a full six years after the thing
was installed in the american embassy in
moscow a

claims.rationale: The accidental British radio-operator tune-in is the standard origin story in Peter Wright's Spycatcher and related histories, though the definitive bug-location sweep came in 1952 under George Kennan. Host's 1951 first-intercept date is consistent with published accounts.

claims.text: During its seven years of operation, the Thing captured top-secret conversations involving General Eisenhower, White House staff, and a dozen congressmen.

evidence.quote:
said the thing
allowed the kgb to get specific and very
important information which gave us
certain advantages in the prediction and
performance

claims.rationale: Spaso House did host high-level guests in this period, and the KGB officer's quoted assessment is from a published account. Specific claim about 'a dozen congressmen' is less verifiable; exact catch list from The Thing's intercepts has never been publicly released.

claims.text: In May 1960, after a Soviet missile shot down an American U-2 spy plane, the Soviets drafted a UN resolution condemning U.S. espionage.

evidence.quote:
n american u2
spy plane was shot down over soviet
airspace a few weeks later at a u.n
national security coun

claims.rationale: Well-documented. Gary Powers's U-2 was shot down May 1, 1960 over Sverdlovsk; the USSR introduced a UN Security Council resolution on May 23, 1960 condemning aggressive overflights. Historical record.

claims.text: In response to Soviet UN accusations of U.S. spying, Ambassador Henry Cabot Lodge publicly displayed the Great Seal bug at the UN, revealing Soviet espionage.

evidence.quote:
great seal presented as a gift by the
russians the thing was top secret for
almost 10 years but now the entire world
was shown spying goes both

claims.rationale: Well-documented UN Security Council moment: Henry Cabot Lodge Jr. brandished the Great Seal during the May 26, 1960 session to counter Soviet U-2 complaints. Host's 'ambassador large' is a transcription corruption of Lodge. Event is UN-recorded.

dependencies.rationale: [alternative] The Soviet accusations of US-only spying are counter-punched by proof of Soviet spying, showing both sides did it.
The specific UN display is the endpoint of the story that began with the 1945 gift.

claims.text: The UN voted 7-2 against the Soviet resolution condemning U.S. espionage, after the Great Seal bug was exposed.

evidence.quote:
]
and here is the
here is the clandestine listening device
uh you can see the antenna in the area
and it was r

claims.rationale: UN Security Council records for May 1960 show the Soviet U-2 resolution was defeated (2 in favor, 7 against, 2 abstentions). Host's '7-2' transposes the vote directionally but matches the loss. The bug-exposure was a factor in the debate.

claims.text: Host's closing framing: the Cold War escalation and global nuclear anxiety were set in motion by a wooden carving delivered by children.

evidence.quote:
ight
the un voted against the soviet
resolution 7-2 and now thanks to a
wooden carving delivered by children as
a gift of peace and friendship the cold
war was in full sw

claims.rationale: Rhetorical summary — real escalation of the Cold War had many causes beyond the Great Seal bug, so the causal attribution is loose. Omitting directTruth because it's a narrative framing, not a verifiable single-cause claim.
